claim: MODIS (Terra and Aqua) and VIIRS satellite instruments detect active agricultural fires at 375m-1km resolution, with NASA FIRMS providing near-real-time fire-alert data and GFED providing emissions estimates including black carbon, organic carbon, and PM2.5 from agricultural burning.

source.title: NASA Fire Information for Resource Management System (FIRMS)

method: Reviewed FIRMS documentation for detection algorithm, spatial resolution, temporal coverage, and data access conditions. Cross-referenced with GFED emissions methodology documentation.
limitations: MODIS 1km resolution misses fires smaller than approximately 100m, systematically underestimating small-field burning common in fragmented agricultural landscapes.
Cloud cover during October-November monsoon withdrawal period blocks satellite detection for days at a time.
VIIRS 375m resolution improves detection but still misses smoldering residue fires with low thermal signature.
GFED emissions estimates depend on burned-area algorithms that have known commission and omission errors in agricultural landscapes.

claim: India's CPCB operates approximately 300 continuous ambient air-quality monitoring stations nationally — roughly one per 4.7 million people — heavily concentrated in urban areas and sparse in the rural Punjab-Haryana agricultural belt where burning occurs.

source.title: Central Pollution Control Board — National Air Quality Monitoring Programme

method: Reviewed CPCB station inventory for geographic distribution, monitoring parameters, and data availability. Mapped station locations against Punjab-Haryana burning districts.
limitations: Station count grows annually; the 300 figure is approximate and may not reflect the most recent additions.
Rural monitoring stations in Punjab and Haryana are extremely sparse relative to the burning area — most districts have zero monitors.
PM2.5 measurement methods vary across stations; not all use the same reference method.
Data access requires registration and download from the CPCB portal; real-time API availability is limited.

claim: The Indian government's crop-residue management program has provided over 200,000 subsidized Happy Seeder machines and other no-burn equipment to farmers, but burning continues at large scale — approximately 50-70 percent of rice residue is still burned in Punjab despite eight years of the program.

source.title: Commission for Air Quality Management — Annual Report on Crop Residue Management

method: Reviewed CAQM annual reports for machine distribution statistics, burning incidence data from VIIRS fire counts, and program expenditure. Cross-referenced with Punjab Agricultural University field studies on Happy Seeder adoption barriers.
limitations: Machine distribution numbers do not equal adoption — many subsidized machines remain underutilized due to narrow planting windows and operational constraints.
Burning percentage estimates from VIIRS fire counts vs. ground surveys differ substantially.
Program reports may understate continued burning to maintain political narrative of progress.
Economic analysis of Happy Seeder cost-effectiveness does not account for all farmer-level constraints including wheat yield penalties reported in some soil conditions.
